questions.question: In Module Federation, why is 'vue' listed in the shared config of both host and remote apps?
options: To reduce bundle size by deduplicating the Vue library
To ensure both apps use the same Vue instance so reactivity and provide/inject work across boundaries
It's required by the federation plugin to resolve component imports
To enable Vue DevTools to connect to both apps simultaneously

questions.question: What is the main advantage of the Web Components approach for micro-frontends over Module Federation?
options: It supports shared Pinia stores between micro-frontends
It provides better TypeScript support across team boundaries
It is framework-agnostic: the shell doesn't need to know it's Vue
It enables Vue DevTools integration across all micro-frontends

questions.question: For most teams, what is the recommended alternative to micro-frontends?
options: Splitting the app into multiple Nuxt projects with shared modules
A well-structured monolith with lazy-loaded routes and clear module boundaries
Using iframes to isolate team boundaries
Building each feature as a separate npm package
